Understanding
Infrastructure

What and Why?

What   i s  i nfr ast r uct ur e?


Interconnection of basic facilities and services enabling an area to
function properly


The term typically refers to the physical and technical structures
that support a society, such as roads, bridges, water supply,
sewers, electrical grids, telecommunications


Infrastructure facilitates     the production of goods & services, and
also the distribution of finished products to markets

Communications and
Networks

Concepts and Protocols

What   exact l y  ar e  communi cat i on  and  net wor ks?


Communications       is the process    of using computing and communication technologies
to transfer  data from one place to another, and vice versa


It enables the movement of electronic or digital           data between two or more nodes,
re g ard l e s s   of   g e og rap hi cal   l ocat i on,   t e chnol og i cal   me d i um  or   d at a  cont e nt s


Networks    link computers and computer devices for flexible processing


They connect     computers and computer equipment in a building, around the country,
or around the world to enable electronic communications

Slide 12
      What   ar e  net wor k  t opol ogi es?


      Building a network involves
      two types of design: logical
      and physical


      A logical model shows how
      the network will be
      organized and arranged                        Bus - A bus network consists of computers and computer
                                                                      devices on a single line

      A physical model describes
      how the hardware and
      software in the network
      will be physically and
      electronically linked.

                                                                                                                      Hierarchical -The hierarchical network
                                                                                                                             uses a tree-like structure.





                                                      A star network has a central hub or computer system

Slide 13
               What   ar e  t he  basi cs  of  net wor ks?


               3 basic generic types

                                                                                                                                                                                              MANs are geographically
                                         LANs interconnect                                                                                                                                    separated but in same
                                         computers in a limited area                                                                                                                          metropol i tan ci ty.
                                         such as a home, school,
                                         computer laboratory, or                                                                                                                              A MAN mi ght be owned
                                         office building using                                                                                                                                and operated by a single
                                         network media.                                                                                                                                       organization, but it usually
                                                                                                                                                                                              wi l l  be used by many
                                                                                                                                                                                              individuals and
                                                                                                                                                                                              organizations. MANs might
                                                                                                                                                                                              also be own e d  a n d
                                                                                                                                                                                              operated as public utilities

                                                                            that links across metropolitan, regional or national boundaries) using private or

               (Baltzan, Lynch & Fisher 2015, p. 161)                       In essence, this mode of telecommunication allows a business to effectively
                                                                            carry out its daily function regardless of location.

Slide 14
      Can we go further…?


      Intranet: An internal network based on Web technologies that allows people within organization
      to exchange information and work on projects


      Extranet: A network based on Web technologies that allows selected outsiders, such as business
      partners and customers, to access authorized resources of the intranet of a company


      Internet: the world’s largest computer network, actually consisting of thousands of
      interconnected networks

Slide 15
        What   ar e  dat a  communi cat i ons  fr amewor ks?


        There are two (2) major data communication frameworks have been developed to
        help ensure that networks meet business and communication requirements:


        Open Systems Interconnection (OSI) re f e re nce   mod e l   d ev e l op e d   by   t he
        International Standards Organization (ISO)


        Tr  a   n   s   m  i   s   s   i   o   n       C   o   n   t   r  o   l       P   r  o   t   o   c   o   l   /   I   n   t   e   r   n   e   t       P   r  o   t   o   c   o   l       (   T C   P   /   I   P   )    suite

How does OSI work i n practi ce?


The 7 layer OSI reference model is used in many ways:
To           p     r   o   v     i     d     e           a     s     s     i     s     t     a     n    c     e           w     h    e     n          t     r   o     u    b     l     e     s     h    o     o     t     i     n    g           n    e     t     w     o     r     k
problems
To           p     r   o   v     i     d     e           a           c     o     m    m    o     n     terminologyand framework for
networking technology developers
To           f     a     c     i     l     i     t     a     t     e           t     h    e           d     e   v     e     l     o     p     m    e     n    t           o     f           c     o     n    n    e     c     t     i     v     i     t     y
standards                      needed for flexible open architectures
To           e     n    a     b     l     e           t     h    e           d     e   v     e     l     o     p     m    e     n    t           o     f           p     r   o     t     o     c     o     l      stacksthat
allow network nodes to communicate with one
another


















                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  Slide 16

How does packet swi tchi ng work?


Method of slicing digital messages into parcels (packets), sending packets along different
communication paths as they become available, and then reassembling packets at destination


Packet switching more efficient use of network’s communications capacity







































(Laudon & Laudon 2014, p.283)
                                                                                                                                     Slide
                                                                                                                                     17

Slide 18
          How does TCP-IP work?


          TC P / I P   ( Tra n s mi s s i o n   C o n t ro l   P ro t o c o l / I n t e r n e t   P ro t o c o l )  is the basic communication language or
          protocol of the Internet


          Yo  u  r       c  o  m  p   u  t   e   r       o  r       d   e v   i   c  e       i   s      p   r o v   i   d   e   d       w  i   t   h      a   copyof the TCP/IP program just as every other
          computer that you may send messages to or get information from also has a copy of TCP/IP


          TC P / I P   i s   a  two-layer program


          The higher layer: Tr  a   n   s   m  i   s   s   i   o   n       C   o   n   t   r  o   l       P   r  o   t   o   c   o   l   ,       m  a   n   a   g   e   s       t   h   e       a   s   s   e   m  b   l   i   n   g       o   f       a       m  e   s   s   a   g   e       o   r       f   i   l   e
          into smaller packets that are transmitted over the Internet and received by a TCP layer that
          re as s e mb l e s   t he   p acke t s   i nt o  t he   or i g i nal   me s s ag e .


          The lower layer: Internet Protocol, handles the address part of each packet so that it gets to the
          right destination.

Slide 19
      What   ar e  I P  ( I nt er net   Pr ot ocol )   Addr esses?


      Every machine on a network has a unique identifier


      There are two standards for IP addresses: IP Version 4 (IPv4) and IP Version 6 (IPv6)


      An IPv4 address is expressed by four numbers separated by dots. Each number is the decimal
      (base-10) representation for an eight-digit binary (base-2) number, also called an octet. For
      ex amp l e :   136. 186. 110. 101


      An IPv6 address is expressed by eight groups of hexadecimal (base-16) numbers separated by
      colons, as in 2001:cdba:0000:0000:0000:0000:3257:9652


      Groups of numbers that contain all zeros are often omitted to save space, leaving a colon
      separator to mark the gap (as in 2001:cdba::3257:9652).

What   i s  Domai n  Name  Syst em  ( DNS) ?


DNS  is a protocol within the set of standards for
how computers exchange data on the Internet
and on many private networks, known as the
TC P / I P   p ro t o c o l   s u i t e


Computers and other network devices on the
Internet use an IP address to rout e       your request
to the site you're trying to reach













                                                                                                                                         Slide
                                                                                                                                         20

Slide 21
      Any other important acronyms?


      URL: Uniform Resource Locator-        It is the global address of documents and other resources on the
      Wo r l d   W i d e   We b


      ISP: Internet Service Provider –    the company/entity that provides you with internet access.


      DNS: Domain Name Server/System -The Domain Name System (DNS) provides visitors access to
      websites using domain names rather than IP addresses


      DHCP: Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP) is a network management protocol used to
      dynamically assign an IP address to any new node entering the network

Slide 22
      What   ar e  por t s?


      In computer networking, a port is an endpoint            of communication in an operating system.


      While the term is also used for physical devices, in software it is a logical construct that identifies
      a specific process or a type of network service


      A port is always associated with an IP address of a host and the protocol type of the
      communication.


      Ports are identified for each protocol and address combination by 16-bit unsigned numbers,
      commonly known as the port number


      Specific port numbers are commonly reserved to identify specific services

Slide 23
      What   ar e  ot her   mai n  i nt er net   pr ot ocol s?


      File Transfer Protocol     (FTP) Data Transfer
      File Transfer Protocol     (FTP) Command Control
      Secure Shell    (SSH) Secure Login
      Te     l     n    e     tre mot e   l og i n  s e r v i ce ,   un-encrypted text messages
      Simple Mail Transfer Protocol        (SMTP) E-mail routing
      Domain Name System          (DNS) service
      Hypertext Transfer Protocol        (HTTP) used in the World Wide Web
      Post Office Protocol     (POP3)
      Network News Transfer Protocol           (NNTP)
      Network Time Protocol        (NTP)
      Internet Message Access Protocol          (IMAP) Management of digital mail
      Simple Network Management Protocol              (SNMP)
      Internet Relay Chat      (IRC)
      HT TP Secure    (HTTPS) HTTP over TLS/SSL

Internet Tech
The hardware and the software

Slide 26
      What   about   soft war e?


      Controls    how the various technology tools work together
      along with the application software


      Operating system software controls the application
      software and manages how the hardware devices work
      together.


      Utility software provides additional functionality to the
      operating system
                                                                                                                                   Note 1:
                                                                                              Application layer now often sits in the cloud
      Application software used for specific information
      processing needs, including payroll, CRM, project                                                                            Note 2:
      management, training etc.                                                                         ‘thick client ’ = app on your device;
                                                                                                             ‘thin client ’ = app in the cloud

Slide 27
      What   i s  Oper at i ng  Syst ems  Soft war e?


      Microsoft Windows ; Most used worldwide; Up to 11; Predecessor MS-DOS


      OS X and iOS Apple Computer Inc.; OS X developed for Macintosh
      computers; iOS for mobile devices e.g. ipad, iphone          etc


      Unix A multi-tasking, multi-user operating system, first developed in
      1969Widely used in servers, workstations and mobile devices


      Linux Unix-like operating system; Embraced by the open-source community
      i.e. No license fee to use it


      Android Smart phone operating system only; based on Linus and other
      open source solutions

Slide 28
      What   i s  Open  Sour ce  Soft war e?


      Source code (human readable) is made available, usually without charge
      Additions and amendments can be made by anyone with the skills
      Provided to the community for others to use
      Software developers are not paid for their work
      Why? Fulfilment; Philosophy; Skill development; Kudos


      VS>


      Closed-source    software, e.g. Microsoft Office
      Only machine-re ad ab l e  code is provided
      Only the original developer can make changes
      Developer depends on license        sales to make money

Slide 29
      What   i s  t he  Cl oud?


      The "cloud" is a set of different types of hardware and software that
      work collectively to deliver many aspects of computing to the end-
      user as an online service


      Cloud computing is the use of hardware and software to deliver a
      service over a network       (typically the Internet)


      With cloud computing, users can access           files and use applications
      from any device that can access the Internet

Business Impacts
Performance and Scaling

Slide 33
      How does al l  thi s i mpact on busi ness?


      Networks and IT infrastructure are mission
      criticalto all modern businesses


      Contemporary corporate network infrastructure
      is a collection of many different networks from
      the Internet, to corporate local area networks
      linking workgroups, departments, or office floors


























 (Laudon & Laudon2014, p.282)

Slide 34
       Why  i s  scal abi l i t y  i mpor t ant ?


       The concept of scalability        is whether the system can
       ex p and   t o  sup p or t   i ncre asi ng  wor kl oad s.


       The scalability of a system, such as a workstation,
       depends on how ex p and ab l e         the that is In this context,
       the words "scalable," "expandable," and "upgradable"
       may be used


       Server scalability is about the system being able serve
       an increased number of requests without faltering or
       lagging (time taken to respond).


       Example: remember trying to book a               covid19                        At what point is this server falling below serving 80% of concurrent connections -4000
       vaccination?

Slide 35
       What   i s  meant   by  Hor i zont al   and  Ver t i cal ?


       “Ve rti cal   Scal ab i l i ty  -UP” -Adding resource within
       the same logical unit to increase capacity. An
       ex amp l e   of  t hi s  woul d   b e   t o  ad d   CP Us  t o  an
       ex i st i ng  se r v e r ,   or   ex p and i ng  st orage   by   ad d i ng
       hard drive on an existing RAID/SAN storage.


       “Horizontal Scalability -       OUT”-    Adding multiple
       logical units of resources and making them work
       as a single unit. Most clustering solutions,
       distributed file systems, load-balancers help you
       with horizontal scalability.
